Mojave Nature by Kurt Buzard


Amphipappus fremontii

Fremont's Chaffbush

A low, rounded shrub characteristic of the Mojave, featuring bright green foliage and dense clusters of small, yellow flower heads. Amphipappus fremontii is a monotypic genus, meaning it is the only species within its genus. It is highly specialized for the harsh conditions of the Mojave Desert, often found growing in the company of creosote bush and bursage. The common name chaffbush refers to the scale-like or chaffy nature of the pappus found on the seeds.

Learn More Details About This Plant

Plant Data

Names and Taxonomy

  • Common Names: fremont's chaffbushchaffbush
  • Synonyms:
  • Family: asteraceae
  • Genus Information: CA 1, NV 1, AZ 1, UT 1

Habit and Size

  • Duration: perennial shrub
  • Habit: erect / mounded
  • Shape: rounded / compact
  • Size: 30–60 cm tall
  • Stem: whitish to straw-colored, highly branched

Leaves

  • Color: bright green to yellowish-green
  • Shape: elliptical to obovate
  • Edge: entire
  • Arrangement: alternate
  • Leaf Retention: drought-deciduous

Flowers and Fruit

  • Sex: hermaphroditic
  • Flower Color: yellow
  • Flower Size: 4–6 mm wide (heads)
  • Number of Petals: 1–2 ray flowers (short), 3–6 disc flowers
  • Bloom: March–June
  • Fruit: cypsela (achene) with a chaffy pappus

Habitat and Growing Conditions

  • Water Use: very low
  • Light: full sun
  • Soil: rocky, alkaline, or gravelly soils
  • Habitat: rocky slopes, limestone outcrops, desert washes

Distribution

  • Distribution: CA, NV, AZ, UT
  • Seen:
  • Elevation: 1000–5000 feet (300–1525 meters)

Observed on a limestone slope in the eastern Mojave.
